1: kata to auto (kata to auto, 'in the same way/together'): the phrase connects to Acts 2:1's homothymadon epi to auto — the mission pattern is consistent: synagogue-first, combined community, word and signs. hōste pisteuēsai ('so that they believed'): the immediate result clause; the word was so effective that faith was the natural outcome. Iconium's divided response (v.4) is the standard pattern of Acts proclamation.
2: hoi de apeithountes Ioudaioi ('the unbelieving Jews'): apeitheo (disobey, refuse to believe) is the Acts term for willful rejection rather than ignorance; the same root as peithō (persuade) with the alpha privative. ekakōsan (embittered, stirred up against): the verb means to harm or embitter — a medical term for the effect of a toxic substance. The pattern of Jewish instigation against Gentile sympathizers recurs at Thessalonica (17:5) and Beroea (17:13).
3: hikapon chronon ('considerable time'): the duration signals genuine community formation, not hit-and-run preaching. parrēsiazomenoi (speaking boldly): the Acts boldness-word for Spirit-empowered testimony. epimartyrounti tō logō tēs charitos autou didōnti sēmeia kai terata ('bearing witness to the word of his grace by granting signs and wonders'): the Lord is the subject who confirms his own word — the apostles are instruments, not the confirming agents themselves.
4: eskhisthē de to plēthos tēs poleōs ('the population of the city was divided'): schizō (split, divide) is used for divided crowds at Jesus's ministry (John 7:43; 9:16; 10:19) and becomes Acts' diagnostic for gospel impact — the word does not pass through neutrally but divides. The division tracks the existing social fault-lines: those aligned with the synagogue opposition vs. those aligned with the apostles.
5: hormē (hormē, 'rush, assault, violent impulse'): the word describes the surge of a mob — the same word used for the Gadarene pig-rush (Mark 5:13) and Acts 19:29. hybrisai kai lithobolēsai (to mistreat and stone): the infinitives describe the planned outcome; they fled before execution, not after. The Gentile-Jewish coalition against the apostles shows that opposition to the gospel unites otherwise antagonistic social groups.
6: synidontes katephygon eis tas poleis tēs Lykaonias ('having become aware, they fled to the cities of Lycaonia'): synidon (become aware together) is the apostles' situational intelligence. Lystra and Derbe are the next mission stations — the flight is also the mission's advance. Luke names the surrounding region (Lykaonian) to orient his geographically-aware reader; Lycaonia was a distinct linguistic and cultural region in the Anatolian plateau.
7: kakeipheudangelizomenoi ēsan ('and there too they were preaching the gospel'): the imperfect tense marks the ongoing character of the proclamation — not a single event but a continuous ministry in the surrounding countryside. Luke's one-verse summary of the Derbe/Lystra initial mission telescopes what must have been weeks of work; the healing episode (vv.8-18) is the signature event that both advances and complicates the mission.
8: achri adynatos en tois posin autou ('lame in his feet'): Luke's description echoes the Lydda healing of Aeneas (9:33) and the Jerusalem temple healing (3:2); the pattern of Acts healing narratives uses congenital disability (ek koilias mētros autou, from his mother's womb) to exclude natural recovery. The lame man sitting (ekathēto) is the posture of one who has never known upright movement.
9: houtos ēkousen tou Paulou lalountos; hos atenisas autō kai idōn hoti echei pistin tou sōthēnai ('he heard Paul speaking; Paul looked at him and saw that he had faith to be healed'): the faith (pistis tou sōthēnai, faith to be saved/healed) is what Paul sees — the same perceptual verb as Peter's gaze at the Jerusalem lame man (3:4). Sōthēnai carries both healing and salvation in a single word.
10: eipen megalē phōnē ('said in a loud voice'): the loud voice is the public, authoritative mode of command — not a whispered prayer but a herald's declaration. anastēthi epi tous podas sou orthon ('stand upright on your feet'): the triple emphatic command (stand/arise + on your feet + straight) leaves no ambiguity. The man's hēlato kai periepatei (he leaped up and walked) mirrors the Jerusalem man's leaping (3:8) — the Diaspora mission reproduces the Jerusalem signs.
11: Lykaonikē phōnē (in the Lycaonian language): Luke's notation that the crowd shouted in their regional dialect signals that the apostles did not immediately understand the worship-danger they were in — they couldn't comprehend the Lycaonian. The misidentification (Barnabas as Zeus, Paul as Hermes) reflects the local religious framework; the missionary who speaks effectively in a foreign tongue may still be heard through a foreign interpretive filter.
12: Barnaban men ekaloun Dia, Paulon de Hermeian ('they called Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes'): Zeus is the chief god, Hermes his messenger-herald. Barnabas is assigned the senior deity role (suggesting he cut a more imposing physical presence, or was older); Paul's assignment to Hermes is ironic — the god of eloquence and divine communication is precisely what Paul has been exercising. The local identification shows the Gentile audience mapping the apostles onto their existing divine category.
13: ho de hiereus tou Dios tou ontos pro tēs poleōs ('the priest of Zeus whose temple was in front of the city'): Zeus Propolis (Zeus of the city-gate) was the patron deity of Lystra; the priest brings the official civic religious response. steimmata (wreaths/garlands): the sacrificial animals and garlands were the full preparation for a public divine welcome-sacrifice. The civic officials are now participating — not a fringe crowd but an official religious act.
14: diarrexantes ta himatia autōn ('tearing their garments'): the gesture of tearing clothes is the OT sign of extreme horror at blasphemy (2 Kgs 18:37; Matt 26:65) — the apostles react to being treated as gods with the same gesture the high priest used at Jesus's supposed blasphemy. The irony is sharp: the ones being worshipped respond as if they had heard the worst possible sacrilege.
15: hēmeis homoiopatheis esmen hymin anthrōpoi ('we too are human beings with the same nature as you'): homoiopatheis (of like passions, same nature) appears only here and Jas 5:17 (Elijah as homoiopathēs). The word insists on full human solidarity — not semi-divine messengers but fellow mortals. The content of the good news is specified: epistrepheite apo toutōn tōn mataōn epi theon zōnta (turn from these empty things to the living God) — the first explicitly Gentile evangelism without OT scaffold.
16: en tais paroichoumenais geneais ('in past generations'): the temporal marker acknowledges a real history of Gentile ignorance that God permitted — not approved but allowed. eiasen panta ta ethnē poreuēsthai tais hodois autōn ('he allowed all nations to go their own ways'): the contrast with Israel (to whom God gave his word and law) is implied; the Gentile nations were not abandoned but left to their own religious systems for a defined period now ending. Paul's Areopagus speech (17:30) develops the same theme.
17: agathourgōn (agathourgōn, 'doing good'): a hapax in the NT, combining agathos (good) and ergon (work). God's providential goodness to the nations — rain, harvests, gladness — was itself a testimony that should have directed them toward the source. empiplan trophēs kai euphrosynēs (filling with food and gladness): the creation-goodness is God's ongoing witness to his character; natural revelation is real if insufficient. The argument from providence is the philosophical entry-point Paul uses with philosophically-formed audiences.
18: molis katepausan tous ochlous tou mē thyein autois ('they barely restrained the crowds from sacrificing to them'): molis (barely, with difficulty) intensifies the scene — the religious enthusiasm of the crowd was so strong that it took sustained verbal effort to prevent the sacrifice. Luke's detail implies the apostles' dismay was complete; the contrast with the response they sought (repentance from idols) and the response they got (divine honors) is Acts' sharpest missiological irony.
19: epelithon lithois ton Paulon eseisan te exō tēs poleōs nomisantes auton tethnanai ('they stoned Paul and dragged him outside the city, thinking he was dead'): the same crowd that barely couldn't worship Paul now stones him. The speed of reversal mirrors Lystra's fickle religious energy; the mob that was preparing sacrifices becomes the mob that prepares an execution. Paul's own catalog of sufferings (2 Cor 11:25: 'once I was stoned') certifies this event's historicity.
20: anastas eisēlthen eis tēn polin ('rising up he went back into the city'): anastas (having risen/gotten up) — the same verb as resurrection proclamation; Luke does not attribute a miracle here, but the reader familiar with the resurrection vocabulary registers the resonance. Whether the stoning was near-lethal or (miraculously) survived, Paul's return to the city the same day it tried to kill him is an act of apostolic courage that confirms the commission of 9:16.
21: euangelisamenoi tēn polin ekeinēn kai mathēteusantes hikanous ('having preached the gospel in that city and made many disciples'): mathēteuō (make disciples) is the Great Commission verb (Matt 28:19); it appears here and in Acts 14:21 as the goal of mission — not conversions counted but disciples formed. The return route (Lystra → Iconium → Antioch) deliberately retraces the dangerous path, visiting the communities that formed in the mission's wake.
22: dei hēmas dia pollōn thlipseōn eiselthein eis tēn basileian tou theou ('we must through many tribulations enter the kingdom of God'): dei (it is necessary) applied to the suffering-path into the kingdom is Paul's compressed theology of the cross-shaped mission. Thlipsis (tribulation, pressure) is not incidental to the Christian life but the appointed road to the kingdom — the same dei that governed Jesus's passion (Luke 9:22) governs his apostles' path.
23: cheirotonesantes de autois kat' ekklēsian presbyterous ('having appointed elders for them in each church'): cheirotoneō (originally to vote by raising the hand, then to appoint) is the ordination verb; it appears for Paul and Barnabas's mission-sending (13:3 uses different vocabulary) but here for their appointment of local leadership. The pattern is: preach → make disciples → appoint elders → commit to the Lord. The kat' ekklēsian (church by church) indicates each local community receives its own eldership.
24: dielthontes tēn Pisidian ēlthon eis tēn Pamphylian ('passing through Pisidia they came into Pamphylia'): Luke's route-tracking is geographical specificity that serves narrative and historical purposes simultaneously — the reader can trace the mission on a map. Pisidia and Pamphylia are successive Roman administrative zones; the journey demonstrates the mission's systematic coverage of the Anatolian interior before returning to base.
25: lalesantes en Pergē ton logon ('having spoken the word in Perga'): Perga is where Mark deserted the mission (13:13); now the apostles preach there on the return — completing what they skipped before. Luke's brevity (lalesantes, having spoken) contrasts with the extended Pisidian Antioch narrative; the return journey consolidates what the outward journey inaugurated without repeating detailed speech-accounts.
26: hothen ēsan paradedomenoi tē chariti tou theou ('where they had been committed to the grace of God'): the Antioch church's sending-prayer committed Paul and Barnabas to the grace of God (not to their own resources); the mission's completion is also its accountability-return. eis to ergon ho eplērōsan ('for the work that they had completed'): plēroō (fulfill, complete) frames the entire first missionary journey as a divinely assigned task brought to completion.
27: anēngeilan hosa epoiēsen ho theos met' autōn ('they reported what God had done with them'): the meta-report is theocentric — God did these things with them, not merely through them; the preposition meta suggests partnership rather than pure instrumentalism. hoti ēnoixen tois ethnesin thyran pisteōs ('that he had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles'): thyra pisteōs (door of faith) — Paul uses the open-door metaphor elsewhere (1 Cor 16:9; 2 Cor 2:12; Col 4:3) for gospel-opportunity.
28: dietribon de chronon ouk oligon syn tois mathētais ('they spent not a little time with the disciples'): ouk oligon (not a little = considerable) is Lukan understatement for what was likely several months. The Antioch home base provides the breathing space between missions; the community life with disciples is itself the mission's fruit — not just individuals converted but a community formed. The Jerusalem council crisis (ch.15) interrupts this interlude.
